finding: The revised legacy heuristic ("an item id is legacy iff its final `-`-delimited segment is entirely digits") misclassifies legitimate CURRENT ids as legacy, so round-2 findings 5 and 18 are NOT resolved — the revision only reworded the rule, it did not fix the underlying soundness problem. §10.3 derives the slug from the item's own subject (lowercased; non-alphanumeric runs -> single `-`), so any subject whose final token is numeric produces a current id whose final `-`-delimited segment is entirely digits: e.g. a checklist item 'verify port 8080' -> slug 'verify-port-8080' -> id 'file-go-safety-verify-port-8080' (final segment '8080') is flagged legacy. The same is true for EVERY collision suffix §10.3 defines: two items sharing slug 'config-validation' yield 'file-go-safety-config-validation' and 'file-go-safety-config-validation-2'; the second's final segment is '2' (digits) -> flagged legacy. When such an id appears in a prior audit round, §10.2/§10.9 mark it 'positional and not comparable', defeating the (role, item_id) cross-round dedup that §10.3 exists to guarantee — the exact failure mode round-2 findings 5 and 18 warned about ('if generation and detection disagree on what an id looks like, dedup and legacy classification both break'). Two implementers can now write contradictory assertions, the precise ambiguity the findings asked to eliminate.
suggestion: Detect legacy by a token the slug scheme cannot synthesize, not by a digit tail. Either (a) stamp a schema/version field on the round record and treat absent-version == pre-release (mirroring v0.25.0's roles_hash 'empty == pre-release' rule), or (b) define legacy strictly as file-<role>-<n> where <n> is a SINGLE trailing integer with no preceding alphabetic slug segment AND the id is not a current slug (checked against the role's current checklist first), and explicitly state that a current collision-suffixed id (-2/-3) or a slug ending in a number is never legacy.

finding: §10.3 appends a numeric collision suffix ('-2', '-3', ...) to the slug, so a collision-resolved current id is 'file-<role>-<slug>-2'. §10.9 classifies an id as legacy iff its final '-'-delimited segment is entirely digits — '2' qualifies. Every collision-resolved current id is therefore misclassified as legacy, which makes §10.2's prior-round section falsely state the ids are positional and not comparable, the exact failure §10.3 was added to eliminate. The collision scheme and the legacy detection rule contradict each other.
suggestion: Make the two rules agree: either change the collision suffix to a non-numeric form (e.g. '_2' or '-dup2') so it cannot satisfy the legacy test, or tighten §10.9 to 'legacy iff the entire tail after file-<role>- is a single integer' so file-go-safety-2 is legacy but file-go-safety-config-validation-2 is current.

finding: §10.9 classifies an item id as legacy (positional) iff its final '-'-delimited segment is entirely digits. But §10.3's collision rule appends '-2','-3',... to a slug, so a *current* id like 'file-go-safety-config-validation-2' has a final segment ('2') that is also entirely digits and is therefore misread as legacy. Every collision-suffixed subject is then flagged 'positional, ids not comparable' in each later round's prior-round section (§10.2) and dropped from cross-round (role,item_id) matching (§10.3) — silently breaking the very stability §10.3 introduced, exactly for the colliding subjects. The detector is circular with the id scheme it is meant to distinguish.
suggestion: Do not infer legacy from the id string. Either restrict the positional form to ^file-<role>-\d+$ (single all-digit segment with NO slug body, so a slug+suffix like config-validation-2 is not matched), or — cleaner — store an explicit per-round id_scheme marker (positional|slug) in the round record (ReviewRound in reviewstate.go) and read that, since §10.9 already commits to not rewriting recorded round files.

finding: §10.2 computes the changed-since flag from 'the prior round stored recorded_at timestamp (read from the round record, not re-derived from git)' and §10.9 treats its absence as the marker of a legacy round — implying the field is new this release. But no section states that this release adds recorded_at to the round-record schema, nor when it is written (at 'tp audit --record'? 'tp review --record' too? wall-clock UTC?). An implementer reading only this spec cannot tell whether recorded_at already exists in .tp-review/ round files or must be added, and by which code path.
suggestion: Add one sentence in §10.2 (or a new subsection): 'tp review --record and tp audit --record stamp each recorded round with recorded_at (wall-clock UTC) at write time; rounds recorded before this release carry none.'

finding: §10.2 omits the changed-since flag 'When the prior round's record carries no usable timestamp (a legacy round, §10.9)', equating 'no usable recorded_at' with 'legacy'. The two are independent: recorded_at is written unconditionally on every record (review_record.go:91 and audit_record.go:106 set RecordedAt = time.Now() with no caveat), and RolesHash is the only field documented as empty for pre-v0.25 rounds. A §10.9 'legacy' round is defined by positional ids (recorded before this release) and still carries a valid recorded_at, so it CAN compute the flag; a round lacking recorded_at is corrupt/hand-edited, not legacy. The parenthetical cross-reference to §10.9 is the wrong definition and would make a correct legacy round print a false 'unavailable' note.
suggestion: Drop the '(a legacy round, §10.9)' gloss. Define the flag-omission condition on its own terms (recorded_at absent/empty/unparseable) and keep §10.9's 'ids not comparable' note tied solely to the id-scheme check, which is an independent concern from timestamp presence.

finding: The slug rule says 'leading and trailing - trimmed; then truncated to at most 40 characters (keeping the trailing -)'. This is self-contradictory — the trailing '-' is trimmed and then kept — and does not say whether truncation cuts mid-word at char 40, snaps back to the last '-' at or before 40, or truncates to 40 then appends a '-'. The chosen reading changes the stored id format (a stable cross-round contract) and how the collision suffix joins ('slug-2' vs 'slug--2').
suggestion: Pick one behavior and state it literally with a worked example covering both the plain and the collision case, e.g. 'truncate at the last - at or before position 40; never append a trailing -'.

finding: §10.9 classifies an item id as legacy (positional) iff its final '-'-delimited segment is entirely digits. §10.3 appends '-2','-3',... on slug collision, so a CURRENT (post-release) id such as file-go-safety-config-validation-2 ends in a digit-only segment ('2') and is misclassified as legacy/positional by the §10.9 rule. A prior-round section built from such a round would then wrongly state its ids are positional and not comparable — precisely when slug collisions (the stress case §10.3 exists for) occur, defeating §10.2's cross-round memory. A tester cannot write a deterministic classification assertion because the §10.9 test contradicts the §10.3 collision format.
suggestion: Disambiguate legacy positional ids from collision suffixes, e.g. legacy iff the final segment is digits AND the segment before it is also an integer index (old file-<role>-<n> shape), or use a non-digit collision suffix (e.g. _2), or key legacy detection off the round record's version rather than the id's final segment.

finding: §5.4 ties the omitted-count message only to the 12-entry total cap ('when entries are dropped the section says how many were omitted — a silent cap reads as "this is everything"'). But Test #4 asserts that --prior 0 'drops recency entries, stating how many were omitted' even though --prior 0 with fewer than 12 dependencies never trips the cap. A tester reading §5.4 alone would emit no omitted message for --prior 0 (no cap hit) and fail Test #4; the spec neither says the omitted-count also fires for --prior-induced exclusions nor defines the count (the default-5 candidate pool vs. the actual number of recent closures available).
suggestion: State explicitly that the omitted-count message fires whenever recency candidates are excluded — by --prior (including --prior 0) or by the 12-entry cap — and define the count as (recency candidates available − recency entries shown).

finding: Round-2 finding 14 asked how amended/rebased commits are handled for the changed-since flag (committer time vs author time vs the recorded_at wall clock). §10.2 now defines recorded_at (read from the round record, not git) and the trigger ('a commit touching that file landed after recorded_at'), but 'landed' does not pin which commit timestamp is compared, so a rebase/amend that rewrites author dates can flip the flag inconsistently across implementations. The recorded_at and data-source sub-points were fixed; this sub-point was not.
suggestion: Pin the comparison to committer time (the moment the commit entered the branch) and state that amended/rebased commits are evaluated by their current committer date.

finding: Round-2 finding 19 sub-point (a) asked to classify duration_source for `tp done --batch` rows that carry an explicit started_at (done.go:729-733). §11.2 enumerates `claimed` sources as tp claim/tp next/tp next --brief and `implicit` as tp done's and tp commit's implicit claim, but never classifies a started_at carried inside a batch row — it was set neither by those claim commands nor by an implicit claim. A batch-closed task whose row supplies started_at therefore has undefined provenance, making its estimation_accuracy bucket non-deterministic. Only the managed-field and reopen-then-implicit sub-points of finding 19 were fixed; this path was not.
suggestion: State that a batch row's carried started_at inherits the provenance of how it was produced (claimed if it originated from an explicit claim, implicit otherwise), or add a third classification for caller-supplied started_at.

finding: A prior-work entry emits commit_files_total alongside the per-entry '+N more' count: when >5 files they encode the same magnitude (total = 5 + N), and when <=5 files total just equals the shown path count. So commit_files_total is always derivable from what is already shown and feeds no decision the agent cannot already make. Reinforced by §15 test #4, which verifies the '+N more' count but never checks commit_files_total — the test treats the +N count as the sole magnitude signal.
suggestion: Drop commit_files_total from the brief's prior-work entry; the path list (<=5) plus the '+N more' count fully convey magnitude. Keep commit_files_total as the stored task field (§6.4) for tp show/report, where the path list is not always shown. Impact is limited to the default (non-compact) path since --compact already drops the whole file payload (§12.1).

finding: §5.2 computes the '+N more' count from the stored commit_files array, which §6.4 caps at 50, while the same entry also carries commit_files_total (the true count). When a task touched more than 50 files the displayed '5 shown + N more' (N at most 45) never sums to commit_files_total — e.g. 5+45 = 50 but commit_files_total = 80 — so a fresh reader cannot reconcile the two numbers and cannot tell how many files were actually omitted.
suggestion: Derive '+N more' from (commit_files_total - 5) rather than (len(commit_files) - 5), or render it as '+N more stored (M total)' so the capped-stored vs true-total distinction is explicit.

finding: --prior declares range 0-20, but the same section hard-caps total entries at 12 with dependency entries always included, so recency is clamped to (12 - dependency count). With 0 dependencies, --prior 13-20 all yield 12 recency entries; with 8 dependencies, any --prior >= 4 yields 4. The advertised upper range is inert — an agent may set --prior 20 and parse output identical to --prior 12, paying tokens for a no-op knob value. The economy cap (round-2) left the previously-accepted range's upper half dead.
suggestion: Align the declared range with the hard cap (range 0-12, out-of-range exits 2 adjusted accordingly), or drop the 12-entry hard cap and let --prior govern recency directly (dependency entries always included regardless). Either removes the dead range without weakening the bound.

finding: --prior is documented with range 0-20 (exit 2 outside it), but §5.4 also imposes a hard total cap of 12 entries. With zero dependency-derived entries, --prior 13 through --prior 20 all yield at most 12 recency rows (the rest dropped with an 'omitted' count), so the upper half of the accepted range can never affect output. The flag's accepted range exceeds its effective range, forcing the implementer to either clamp silently or special-case values above 12.
suggestion: Either narrow the accepted range to 0-12 to match the cap, or state explicitly that values above 12 are clamped to the 12-entry section cap so both the exit-2 boundary and the clamp are documented.
